metadata.summary: Per-project environment management with direnv (auto-loads .envrc) and mise (runtime manager replacing pyenv/nvm/asdf). Pattern: `use mise` + `layout python-venv .venv` in .envrc.

content.title: direnv + mise Version Management
content.summary: Per-project environment management on Ubuntu 24.04 with direnv (auto-loads .envrc on directory entry) and mise (polyglot runtime manager replacing pyenv/nvm/asdf). Key pattern: `use mise` + `layout python-venv .venv` in .envrc auto-activates the correct Python version and virtualenv when entering a project directory. Shell integration order is critical: mise hook must come BEFORE direnv hook in .bashrc.

why.p: Servers running multiple Python projects need isolated runtimes — Python 3.11 for one project, 3.12 for another, without manual `source .venv/bin/activate`. direnv automates activation and de-activation on directory change. mise replaces three separate tools (pyenv, nvm, asdf) with one binary and integrates cleanly with direnv via `use mise`.

list.item: Multiple Python/Node projects on the same server needing different runtime versions
Automatically activating virtualenvs when entering project directories
Loading project-specific .env variables securely without shell leakage
Setting up a new development server with reproducible runtime environments
Single-project servers where a single system Python or one venv is sufficient
Managed platforms (Heroku, Railway) that provide runtime isolation at the container level
When the project already uses Docker for isolation — runtime pinning is handled in the image
Production systemd services — pin the venv path explicitly in ExecStart, don't rely on direnv

rule.statement: Run `direnv allow` once in every directory with a new or modified .envrc before it executes.
rule.rationale: direnv runs arbitrary shell code from .envrc. Without explicit trust, cloning a repo with a malicious .envrc would execute code on cd. This is the key security mechanism.

section.name: mise-as-asdf-replacement
section.p: mise replaces pyenv, nvm, and asdf with a single tool. Reads .tool-versions (asdf-compatible) or mise.toml. Integrates with direnv via `use mise` directive in .envrc.

list.item: `mise install python@3.12.8` — download and compile Python 3.12.8
`mise use python@3.12.8` — set version for current directory (writes .tool-versions)
`mise current` — show active versions in current directory

rule.statement: mise hook must appear BEFORE direnv hook in .bashrc. direnv must be the LAST hook in .bashrc.
rule.rationale: direnv re-evaluates the shell environment on every directory change. If mise activates after direnv, the runtime versions set by mise won't be visible to direnv's `use mise` directive.

example.code: # ~/.bashrc — correct order eval "$(mise activate bash)" # 1. mise first eval "$(direnv hook bash)" # 2. direnv LAST
# Wrong order eval "$(direnv hook bash)" eval "$(mise activate bash)"
# .envrc use mise layout python-venv .venv dotenv_if_exists .env watch_file requirements.txt watch_file pyproject.toml PATH_add scripts export PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE=1 export PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1

list.item: `layout python-venv .venv` — create .venv with current Python, activate

template.purpose: Shell hook lines for .bashrc: mise activate before direnv hook
Full .envrc for Python project: use mise, layout python-venv, dotenv, watch_file
.envrc for Node project: use mise, layout node, dotenv
mise-native config with tools, env vars, and task definitions
